preamble.summary: Proverbs 16 marks a shift in the Solomonic collection. The first half (verses 1-9) concentrates an unusually dense cluster of 'Yahweh proverbs' — statements about God's sovereignty over human plans, speech, and outcomes. The second half returns to the standard topics of kingship, pride, speech, and the contrast between wisdom and folly. This chapter contains some of the most theologically charged statements in the entire book.
preamble.remarkable: The opening nine verses form the most sustained meditation on divine sovereignty in Proverbs. The human heart plans, but the LORD directs the tongue (verse 1), evaluates motives (verse 2), establishes plans when committed to Him (verse 3), has made everything for its purpose (verse 4), detests arrogance (verse 5), and directs a person's steps (verse 9). This sequence systematically dismantles human autonomy without destroying human responsibility — the person still plans, still speaks, still acts, but God is the one who determines outcomes. Verse 25 repeats 14:12 verbatim: 'There is a way that seems right to a person, but its end is the ways of death.'
preamble.friction: Verse 4 — 'The LORD has made everything for its purpose, even the wicked for the day of trouble' — is one of the most theologically difficult statements in the Hebrew Bible. It can be read as affirming that God has a purpose even for the existence of evil, or more modestly that the wicked will meet their proper end on the day of judgment. The sages do not resolve the tension; they state it and leave it.
preamble.connections: The divine-sovereignty cluster in verses 1-9 connects to Jeremiah 10:23 ('I know, LORD, that a person's way is not in their own hands') and James 4:13-15 ('If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that'). Verse 6 — 'by faithful love and truth sin is atoned for' — echoes the chesed ve-emet pairing from Exodus 34:6. The repetition of 14:12 in verse 25 creates a structural echo across the collection.

verses.text_hebrew: לְאָדָ֥ם מַעַרְכֵי־לֵ֑ב וּ֝מֵיְהוָ֗ה מַעֲנֵ֥ה לָשֽׁוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: The preparations of the heart in man, and the answer of the tongue, is from the LORD.
verses.rendering:
A person may arrange his thoughts,
but the answer of the tongue comes from the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: The le-adam ma'arkhe lev ('to a person belong the arrangements of the heart') — humans have the capacity to plan, to organize their thoughts, to prepare what they want to say. But u-me-Yahweh ma'aneh lashon ('and from the LORD comes the tongue's answer') — what actually comes out of the mouth, the words that are actually spoken in the critical moment, is under God's governance. The proverb does not negate human planning but subordinates it: you prepare, but God delivers. This verse launches a sustained sequence on divine sovereignty that dominates the chapter's opening.
translator_notes: The ma'arkhe lev ('arrangements of the heart') uses military language — ma'arakhah is a battle formation. The human heart lines up its thoughts like troops, but God controls the actual engagement. The gap between preparation and performance belongs to God.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־דַּרְכֵי־אִ֭ישׁ זַ֣ךְ בְּעֵינָ֑יו וְתֹכֵ֖ן רוּח֣וֹת יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: All the ways of a man are clean in his own eyes; but the LORD weigheth the spirits.
verses.rendering:
All a person's ways seem clean to him,
but the LORD weighs the motives.
translator_notes: Kol darkhe ish zakh be-einav ('all a person's ways are pure in his own eyes') — self-assessment is unreliable because self-interest distorts perception. But tokhen ruchot Yahweh ('the LORD weighs the spirits/motives') — God evaluates the ruach, the inner drive and intention behind the visible behavior. Human beings see surfaces; God calibrates depths.

verses.text_hebrew: גֹּ֣ל אֶל־יְהוָ֣ה מַעֲשֶׂ֑יךָ וְ֝יִכֹּ֗נוּ מַחְשְׁבֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Commit thy works unto the LORD, and thy thoughts shall be established.
verses.rendering:
Commit your actions to the LORD,
and your plans will be established.
translator_notes: Gol el Yahweh ma'asekha ('roll upon the LORD your works') — the verb gol means to roll a burden off one's own shoulders and onto someone else's. It is an act of trust: placing the weight of one's undertakings onto God. The result is ve-yikkonu machshevotekha ('and your plans will be established, made firm'). Surrender to God does not cancel planning but secures it.

verses.text_hebrew: כֹּ֣ל פָּ֭עַל יְהוָ֣ה לַמַּעֲנֵ֑הוּ וְגַם־רָ֝שָׁ֗ע לְי֣וֹם רָעָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD hath made all things for himself: yea, even the wicked for the day of evil.
verses.rendering:
The LORD has made everything for its purpose,
even the wicked for the day of trouble.
verses.expanded_rendering: Kol pa'al Yahweh la-ma'anehu ('the LORD has made everything for its answer/purpose/response') — everything in creation has a function within God's design. The statement is comprehensive and unyielding. Then the difficult extension: ve-gam rasha le-yom ra'ah ('and even the wicked for the day of evil/trouble'). This does not necessarily mean God created people to be wicked, but rather that even the wicked have a place in God's ordering of reality — specifically, they will meet their appropriate end on the day of reckoning. The wicked are not outside God's sovereignty; even they serve a purpose within the larger design, though that purpose includes their own judgment.
translator_notes: The la-ma'anehu is ambiguous — it could mean 'for its own purpose' or 'for His purpose' (God's). The ambiguity may be intentional. The verse pushes the reader toward trust in God's governance even when evil seems purposeless.
This verse has generated enormous theological discussion. It does not resolve the problem of evil but asserts that God's sovereignty encompasses it.

verses.text_hebrew: תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה כׇּל־גְּבַהּ־לֵ֑ב יָ֥ד לְ֝יָ֗ד לֹ֣א יִנָּקֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Every one that is proud in heart is an abomination to the LORD: though hand join in hand, he shall not be unpunished.
verses.rendering:
Everyone with an arrogant heart is detestable to the LORD;
be assured, he will not go unpunished.
translator_notes: Kol gevah lev ('everyone lifted up of heart, everyone with an arrogant heart') is to'avat Yahweh. The idiom yad le-yad ('hand to hand') means 'you can count on it, certainly, without a doubt.' The proud person will lo yinnaqeh ('not be held innocent, not go unpunished'). Pride is the sin God most consistently and emphatically condemns in Proverbs.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּחֶ֣סֶד וֶ֭אֱמֶת יְכֻפַּ֣ר עָוֺ֑ן וּבְיִרְאַ֥ת יְ֝הוָ֗ה ס֣וּר מֵרָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: By mercy and truth iniquity is purged: and by the fear of the LORD men depart from evil.
verses.rendering:
By faithful love and truth wrongdoing is atoned for,
and by the fear of the LORD one turns from evil.
translator_notes: Be-chesed ve-emet yekhupar avon ('by faithful love and truth iniquity is covered/atoned for') — the word yekhupar uses the language of cultic atonement (kipper, 'to cover, to make atonement'). The claim is remarkable: the divine attributes of chesed and emet, when expressed in human relationships, have atoning power. And be-yir'at Yahweh sur me-ra ('by the fear of the LORD one turns from evil') — reverence for God is the engine of moral change.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love and reliability, covenant devotion and truthfulness
key_terms.note: This word pair describes God's own character in Exodus 34:6 and is here presented as having atoning power when practiced by human beings. It suggests that moral transformation happens through covenant loyalty, not just through ritual.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּרְצ֣וֹת יְ֭הוָה דַּרְכֵי־אִ֑ישׁ גַּם־אוֹיְבָ֝֗יו יַשְׁלִ֥ם אִתּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: When a man's ways please the LORD, he maketh even his enemies to be at peace with him.
verses.rendering:
When the LORD is pleased with a person's ways,
He makes even his enemies live at peace with him.
translator_notes: Bi-rtsot Yahweh darke ish ('when the LORD delights in a person's ways') gam oyvav yashlim itto ('even his enemies He makes to be at peace with him'). The verb yashlim is from the same root as shalom — God establishes peace even between adversaries when He favors a person's conduct. This is not a guarantee of universal popularity but a statement about God's power to transform hostile relationships.

verses.text_hebrew: טֹ֣וב מְ֭עַט בִּצְדָקָ֑ה מֵרֹ֥ב תְּ֝בוּאֹ֗ות בְּלֹ֣א מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: Better is a little with righteousness than great revenues without right.
verses.rendering:
Better a little gained with righteousness
than great income without justice.
translator_notes: Another 'better than' proverb echoing 15:16-17. Me'at bi-tsedaqah ('a little with righteousness') outweighs rov tevu'ot be-lo mishpat ('abundant income without justice'). The comparison is not between rich and poor but between legitimate and illegitimate gain. Integrity determines the true value of income.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ב אָ֭דָם יְחַשֵּׁ֣ב דַּרְכּ֑וֹ וַ֝יהוָ֗ה יָכִ֥ין צַעֲדֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A man's heart deviseth his way: but the LORD directeth his steps.
verses.rendering:
A person's heart plans his course,
but the LORD directs his steps.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse completes the sovereignty sequence that opened the chapter. Lev adam yechashev darkko ('the heart of a person calculates his way') — humans plan, deliberate, chart their course. Va-Yahweh yakhin tsa'ado ('but the LORD establishes his step') — the actual steps taken, the real trajectory of a life, is under God's direction. The proverb holds both realities simultaneously: human planning is real, and divine direction is real. The resolution is not that planning is pointless but that it must be held loosely, with the recognition that God's purposes will ultimately prevail over human calculations.
translator_notes: This verse is the capstone of verses 1-9. The progression moves from thought (verse 1) through motive (verse 2), commitment (verse 3), purpose (verse 4), pride (verse 5), atonement (verse 6), favor (verse 7), and justice (verse 8) to arrive here: God is sovereign over the actual path a life takes. Human planning is the pencil sketch; God draws in ink.

verses.text_hebrew: קֶ֭סֶם עַל־שִׂפְתֵ֣י מֶ֑לֶךְ בְּ֝מִשְׁפָּ֗ט לֹ֣א יִמְעַל־פִּֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: A divine sentence is in the lips of the king: his mouth transgresseth not in judgment.
verses.rendering:
An oracle is on the lips of the king;
his mouth must not betray justice.
translator_notes: Qesem ('oracle, divination, authoritative pronouncement') describes the king's speech as carrying the weight of divine authority. Be-mishpat lo yim'al piv ('in judgment his mouth must not act unfaithfully') — this is both description and prescription. The king's words carry oracular power, which makes his responsibility for justice absolute.

verses.text_hebrew: פֶּ֣לֶס וּמֹאזְנֵ֣י מִשְׁפָּ֣ט לַיהוָ֑ה מַ֝עֲשֵׂ֗הוּ כׇּל־אַבְנֵי־כִֽיס׃
verses.text_kjv: A just weight and balance are the LORD's: all the weights of the bag are his work.
verses.rendering:
Honest scales and balances belong to the LORD;
all the weights in the bag are His work.
translator_notes: Peles u-mo'azne mishpat la-Yahweh ('a scale and balances of justice belong to the LORD') — God owns the standard of fair measurement. Ma'asehu kol avne kis ('His work are all the stones of the bag') — every weight-stone used in commerce is ultimately God's creation. Commercial honesty is a theological matter because God made the tools of measurement and claims them as His own.

verses.text_hebrew: תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת מְ֭לָכִים עֲשׂ֣וֹת רֶ֑שַׁע כִּ֥י בִ֝צְדָקָ֗ה יִכּ֥וֹן כִּסֵּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: It is an abomination to kings to commit wickedness: for the throne is established by righteousness.
verses.rendering:
Wicked conduct is detestable to kings,
for a throne is established by righteousness.
translator_notes: To'avat melakhim asot resha ('an abomination to kings is doing wickedness') — not merely that kings should avoid wickedness but that they should find it repulsive. The reason: ki vi-tsedaqah yikkon kisse ('for by righteousness a throne is made firm'). Stability of governance depends on moral integrity. Wickedness undermines the very foundation of royal authority.

verses.text_hebrew: רְצ֣וֹן מְ֭לָכִים שִׂפְתֵי־צֶ֑דֶק וְדֹבֵ֖ר יְשָׁרִ֣ים יֶאֱהָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Righteous lips are the delight of kings; and they love him that speaketh right.
verses.rendering:
Righteous speech delights kings,
and they love the one who speaks honestly.
translator_notes: Retson melakhim siftei tsedeq ('the delight of kings is lips of righteousness') — good kings value honest advisors. Dover yesharim ye'ehav ('and the one who speaks upright things he loves'). The court setting is implied: wise rulers surround themselves with truthful counselors rather than flatterers.

verses.text_hebrew: חֲמַת־מֶ֭לֶךְ מַלְאֲכֵי־מָ֑וֶת וְאִ֖ישׁ חָכָ֣ם יְכַפְּרֶֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: The wrath of a king is as messengers of death: but a wise man will pacify it.
verses.rendering:
A king's fury is a messenger of death,
but a wise person can appease it.
translator_notes: Chamat melekh ('the wrath of a king') is mal'akhe mavet ('messengers of death') — royal anger can be lethal. But ish chakham yekhapperennah ('a wise person can atone for it, can cover it, can pacify it'). The verb kipper (from which Yom Kippur derives) means to cover or deflect — the wise person knows how to de-escalate royal rage.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאוֹר־פְּנֵי־מֶ֥לֶךְ חַיִּ֑ים וּ֝רְצוֹנ֗וֹ כְּעָ֣ב מַלְק֥וֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: In the light of the king's countenance is life; and his favour is as a cloud of the latter rain.
verses.rendering:
In the light of a king's face there is life,
and his favor is like a cloud of spring rain.
translator_notes: Be-or pene melekh chayyim ('in the light of the king's face is life') — the king's smile signals safety and prosperity. Retsono ke-av malqosh ('his favor is like a cloud of the latter rain') — the malqosh is the spring rain that comes just before harvest, essential for the final ripening of crops. Royal favor is as life-giving as the rain that completes the agricultural cycle.

verses.text_hebrew: קְֽנֹה־חׇכְמָ֗ה מַה־טּ֥וֹב מֵחָר֑וּץ וּקְנ֥וֹת בִּ֝ינָ֗ה נִבְחָ֥ר מִכָּֽסֶף׃
verses.text_kjv: How much better is it to get wisdom than gold! and to get understanding rather to be chosen than silver!
verses.rendering:
How much better to acquire wisdom than gold!
To gain understanding is preferable to silver.
translator_notes: Qenoh chokhmah mah tov me-charuts ('acquiring wisdom — how much better than gold!'). The exclamation mah tov ('how good!') expresses genuine enthusiasm. Qenot binah nivchar mi-kasef ('gaining understanding is to be chosen over silver'). Wisdom and understanding are presented as commodities — they can be 'acquired' and 'gained' — but they outvalue precious metals.

verses.text_hebrew: מְסִלַּ֣ת יְ֭שָׁרִים ס֣וּר מֵרָ֑ע שֹׁמֵ֥ר נַ֝פְשׁ֗וֹ נֹצֵ֥ר דַּרְכּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The highway of the upright is to depart from evil: he that keepeth his way preserveth his soul.
verses.rendering:
The highway of the upright turns away from evil;
whoever guards his path preserves his life.
translator_notes: Mesillat yesharim ('the highway of the upright') is sur me-ra ('turning aside from evil') — the upright person's road is defined by what it avoids. Shomer nafsho notser darkko ('the one who guards his life preserves his way') — self-preservation and moral vigilance are the same activity.

verses.text_hebrew: לִפְנֵי־שֶׁ֥בֶר גָּא֑וֹן וְלִפְנֵ֖י כִשָּׁל֣וֹן גֹּ֣בַהּ רֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Pride goeth before destruction, and an haughty spirit before a fall.
verses.rendering:
Before destruction comes arrogance,
and before a fall comes a haughty spirit.
verses.expanded_rendering: Lifne shever ga'on ('before breaking/destruction — arrogance') and lifne khishalon govah ruach ('before stumbling/falling — a lofty spirit'). This is perhaps the most famous proverb in the chapter, commonly paraphrased as 'pride goes before a fall.' The Hebrew is more precise: it is not that pride sometimes leads to destruction but that pride always precedes it. Arrogance is the advance party that arrives before the catastrophe. The govah ruach ('height of spirit, lofty spirit') is not noble aspiration but inflated self-regard — the belief that one is above the constraints that apply to everyone else. That belief is the gravitational setup for the fall.
translator_notes: The parallelism pairs shever ('shattering, destruction') with kishalon ('stumbling, tripping, falling') and ga'on ('arrogance, pride') with govah ruach ('height of spirit'). Both lines say the same thing with intensifying imagery: pride is the precondition for collapse.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֣וֹב שְׁפַל־ר֭וּחַ אֶת־(עֲנָוִ֑ים) [עֲנִיִּ֑ים] מֵחַלֵּ֥ק שָׁ֝לָ֗ל אֶת־גֵּאִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Better it is to be of an humble spirit with the lowly, than to divide the spoil with the proud.
verses.rendering:
Better to be humble in spirit among the lowly
than to divide plunder with the proud.
translator_notes: Tov shefal ruach et aniyyim ('good is lowliness of spirit with the poor/afflicted') me-challeq shalal et ge'im ('than dividing spoil with the arrogant'). The proverb directly follows verse 18's warning about pride. If pride precedes destruction, then humility among the lowly is the safer — and morally superior — position, even if dividing spoils with the proud is more lucrative.

verses.text_hebrew: מַשְׂכִּ֣יל עַל־דָּ֭בָר יִמְצָא־ט֑וֹב וּבוֹטֵ֖חַ בַּיהוָ֣ה אַשְׁרָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: He that handleth a matter wisely shall find good: and whoso trusteth in the LORD, happy is he.
verses.rendering:
Whoever is attentive to a matter will find good,
and whoever trusts in the LORD — how fortunate he is.
translator_notes: Maskil al davar ('one who gives attention to a matter, who handles a situation with insight') yimtsa tov ('will find good'). Bote'ach ba-Yahweh ashrav ('the one who trusts in the LORD — his blessednesses, how fortunate he is'). Practical competence and theological trust are presented as complementary, not competing, sources of well-being.

verses.text_hebrew: לַחֲכַם־לֵ֭ב יִקָּרֵ֣א נָב֑וֹן וּמֶ֥תֶק שְׂ֝פָתַ֗יִם יֹסִ֥יף לֶֽקַח׃
verses.text_kjv: The wise in heart shall be called prudent: and the sweetness of the lips increaseth learning.
verses.rendering:
The wise in heart is called discerning,
and pleasant speech increases persuasiveness.
translator_notes: The chakham lev ('wise of heart') earns the reputation navon ('discerning'). And meteq sefatayim ('sweetness of lips, pleasant speech') yosif leqach ('adds to learning/persuasiveness'). How something is said affects how much is received. The wise person's content and delivery work together.

verses.text_hebrew: מְק֣וֹר חַ֭יִּים שֵׂ֣כֶל לִבְעָלָ֑יו וּמוּסַ֖ר אֱוִלִ֣ים אִוֶּֽלֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Understanding is a wellspring of life unto him that hath it: but the instruction of fools is folly.
verses.rendering:
Insight is a fountain of life for the one who has it,
but the discipline of fools is foolishness.
translator_notes: Sekhel ('insight, good sense') is meqor chayyim ('fountain of life') for its possessors — it sustains them the way a spring sustains a community. But musar evilim ivvelet ('the discipline of fools is foolishness') — fools teach each other only how to be more foolish. The correction they give is as empty as their understanding.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ב חָ֭כָם יַשְׂכִּ֣יל פִּ֑יהוּ וְעַל־שְׂ֝פָתָ֗יו יֹסִ֥יף לֶֽקַח׃
verses.text_kjv: The heart of the wise teacheth his mouth, and addeth learning to his lips.
verses.rendering:
The heart of the wise instructs his mouth
and adds persuasiveness to his speech.
translator_notes: Lev chakham yaskil pihu ('the heart of the wise makes his mouth prudent') — wisdom in the heart governs what comes out of the mouth. Ve-al sefatav yosif leqach ('and to his lips adds learning/persuasiveness'). Internal wisdom produces external eloquence — the two are connected, not separate.

verses.text_hebrew: צ֣וּף דְּ֭בַשׁ אִמְרֵי־נֹ֑עַם מָת֥וֹק לַ֝נֶּ֗פֶשׁ וּמַרְפֵּ֥א לָעָֽצֶם׃
verses.text_kjv: Pleasant words are as an honeycomb, sweet to the soul, and health to the bones.
verses.rendering:
Pleasant words are a honeycomb —
sweet to the soul and healing to the bones.
translator_notes: Tsuf devash imre no'am ('a honeycomb of honey are words of pleasantness') — honeycomb is the purest, most natural form of sweetness. Matoq la-nefesh ('sweet to the soul/appetite') u-marpe la-atsem ('and healing to the bone'). Kind speech nourishes the inner person and restores the body. The sages took the healing power of words literally, not just metaphorically.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵ֤שׁ דֶּ֣רֶךְ יָ֭שָׁר לִפְנֵי־אִ֑ישׁ וְ֝אַחֲרִיתָ֗הּ דַּרְכֵי־מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: There is a way that seemeth right unto a man, but the end thereof are the ways of death.
verses.rendering:
There is a path that seems straight to a person,
but its end is the ways of death.
translator_notes: This verse is identical to 14:12 — the only exact repetition in the Solomonic collection. The sages considered this warning important enough to state twice. Self-assessed correctness is unreliable; what looks right can lead to death. The repetition functions as a structural refrain, reminding the reader that human judgment requires external correction.

verses.text_hebrew: נֶ֣פֶשׁ עָ֭מֵל עָ֣מְלָה לּ֑וֹ כִּֽי־אָכַ֖ף עָלָ֣יו פִּֽיהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that laboureth laboureth for himself; for his mouth craveth it of him.
verses.rendering:
The appetite of a worker works for him,
for his hunger drives him on.
translator_notes: Nefesh amel amlah lo ('the appetite of a toiler toils for him') — hunger is the engine of labor. Ki akhaf alav pihu ('for his mouth bends upon him, presses him, urges him') — the mouth's demands drive the body to work. The proverb is not cynical but observational: physical need is a powerful motivator.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֣ישׁ בְּ֭לִיַּעַל כֹּרֶ֣ה רָעָ֑ה וְעַל־(שְׂפָתוֹ) [שְׂ֝פָת֗וֹ] כְּאֵ֣שׁ צָרָֽבֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: An ungodly man diggeth up evil: and in his lips there is as a burning fire.
verses.rendering:
A worthless person digs up evil,
and on his lips is something like a scorching fire.
translator_notes: Ish beliyya'al ('a man of worthlessness, a scoundrel') koreh ra'ah ('digs up evil') — he excavates trouble, actively searching for ways to cause harm. Al sefato ke-esh tsarevet ('on his lip like a fire that scorches') — his speech burns everything it touches.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֣ישׁ תַּ֭הְפֻּכוֹת יְשַׁלַּ֣ח מָד֑וֹן וְ֝נִרְגָּ֗ן מַפְרִ֥יד אַלּֽוּף׃
verses.text_kjv: A froward man soweth strife: and a whisperer separateth chief friends.
verses.rendering:
A perverse person sows conflict,
and a whisperer separates close friends.
translator_notes: Ish tahpukhot ('person of perversions, crooked person') yeshallach madon ('sends out strife, sows quarreling'). The nirgan ('whisperer, gossip') mafrid alluf ('separates a close friend, divides an intimate companion'). Gossip is singled out as uniquely destructive to human bonds — it can sever the closest friendships.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֣ישׁ חָ֭מָס יְפַתֶּ֣ה רֵעֵ֑הוּ וְ֝הוֹלִיכ֗וֹ בְּדֶ֣רֶךְ לֹא־טֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: A violent man enticeth his neighbour, and leadeth him into the way that is not good.
verses.rendering:
A violent person lures his neighbor
and leads him down a path that is not good.
translator_notes: The ish chamas ('person of violence') yefatteh re'ehu ('entices his neighbor, seduces his companion'). The verb patah ('to entice, to seduce, to deceive') suggests manipulation rather than force — the violent person draws others in through false promises before revealing the true nature of the road.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹצֶ֣ה עֵ֭ינָיו לַחְשֹׁ֣ב תַּהְפֻּכ֑וֹת קֹרֵ֥ץ שְׂ֝פָתָ֗יו כִּלָּ֥ה רָעָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He shutteth his eyes to devise froward things: moving his lips he bringeth evil to pass.
verses.rendering:
Whoever narrows his eyes is plotting perversion;
whoever purses his lips brings evil to completion.
translator_notes: The body language of the schemer: otseh einav ('closing/narrowing his eyes') to plan tahpukhot ('perversions, crooked schemes'), and qorets sefatav ('pinching/pursing his lips') as he killah ra'ah ('completes evil, brings harm to fruition'). The sages were acute observers of nonverbal communication — they could read conspiracy in facial expressions.

verses.text_hebrew: עֲטֶ֣רֶת תִּ֭פְאֶרֶת שֵׂיבָ֑ה בְּדֶ֥רֶךְ צְ֝דָקָ֗ה תִּמָּצֵֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: The hoary head is a crown of glory, if it be found in the way of righteousness.
verses.rendering:
Gray hair is a crown of splendor;
it is found on the path of righteousness.
translator_notes: Ateret tif'eret sevah ('a crown of beauty/splendor is gray hair') — old age is honored, not despised. But the qualifying clause matters: be-derekh tsedaqah timmatse ('on the path of righteousness it is found'). The crown is not automatic — it belongs to those who have lived rightly. Gray hair earned through righteous living is beautiful; gray hair alone is merely aging.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֣וֹב אֶ֭רֶךְ אַפַּ֣יִם מִגִּבּ֑וֹר וּמֹשֵׁ֥ל בְּ֝רוּח֗וֹ מִלֹּכֵ֥ד עִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is slow to anger is better than the mighty; and he that ruleth his spirit than he that taketh a city.
verses.rendering:
Better a patient person than a warrior,
and one who controls his temper than one who captures a city.
verses.expanded_rendering: Tov erekh appayim mi-gibbor ('better is the slow-to-anger than a warrior') — patience outranks military prowess. U-moshel be-rucho mi-lokhed ir ('and one who rules his spirit than one who captures a city') — self-mastery is a greater conquest than military conquest. The comparison is deliberate and provocative: in a culture that celebrated warriors and city-takers, the sages insisted that the person who can govern his own emotions has achieved something more difficult and more valuable. Self-control is the highest form of strength.
translator_notes: The gibbor ('warrior, hero, mighty man') was the most admired figure in ancient Near Eastern culture. To say that patience surpasses him is a radical revaluation of strength. The proverb does not denigrate military courage but elevates emotional mastery above it.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּ֭חֵיק יוּטַ֣ל אֶת־הַגּוֹרָ֑ל וּ֝מֵיְהוָ֗ה כׇּל־מִשְׁפָּטֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD.
verses.rendering:
The lot is cast into the lap,
but every decision belongs to the LORD.
translator_notes: Ba-cheiq yutal et ha-goral ('into the lap the lot is thrown') — casting lots was a standard method of decision-making in the ancient world, used when human wisdom had reached its limits. U-me-Yahweh kol mishpato ('and from the LORD is all its judgment/decision') — even the apparently random fall of the lot is under God's governance. The chapter that began with divine sovereignty over human speech (verse 1) closes with divine sovereignty over chance itself. Nothing falls outside God's jurisdiction.
